Monday: Margaret challenged the team on jalapeño corn dog bites, questioning whether it's "nostalgia dressed up as food or if we actually respect the technique here." Marcus and Stephanie identified the real story: the cornmeal crust seals in the pepper's moisture, concentrating heat on bite. They decided to anchor the copy around this moisture-seal engineering angle rather than treating it as decoration.

Tuesday: Margaret established the cornmeal-to-flour ratio at 60-40 minimum to prevent seal breakdown during frying. Marcus and Stephanie agreed the copy should respect this technical constraint rather than apologize for it. Margaret: "Marcus gets it. Fine. Cornmeal-to-flour ratio is 60-40 minimum or the seal breaks down in the oil." Team decided to move forward with this approach.

Wednesday: Julian presented three lighting takes of the jalapeño corn dog bite-through moment. Stephanie pushed for shot two, emphasizing the crisp exterior shattering effect, saying "Shot two's the one - if people are stopping their scroll, it's because they see that crisp exterior breaking apart, right?" Margaret agreed, and the team locked in shot two as the final choice.

Thursday: Marcus finalized copy emphasizing how cornmeal's moisture-seal properties affect texture. Stephanie loved the approach but wanted tighter writing to avoid repetition. Margaret and Stephanie agreed to lead with the science of steam absorption, then show the frying results - Marcus will send the revised version focusing on what happens rather than explaining why.

Friday: Margaret locked Shot Two and copy, inviting final feedback. The team agreed that trusting the work over overthinking improved it - Marcus noted the science-first approach let writing breathe, while Julian credited restraint in cinematography. Stephanie highlighted that listening instead of defending positions made the difference. No disagreements emerged.

Saturday: Devon completed verification and pushed the Jalapeño Corn Dog Bites project live. Margaret and Devon agreed that restraint and clean architecture were key to the product's success. No disagreements or unresolved questions emerged.
